narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid middle-income family background and plans to pursue full-time studies, you've put real effort into your law school application, and I appreciate how challenging this process can feel. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.60 sits below the typical 3.21 average for students in our dataset, while your LSAT score of 28 is notably lower than the usual 36.70—key metrics that reflect your academic journey so far. You performed respectably in school, ranking in the 7th decile for both your first-year and third-year grades, which is above the averages of 5.71 and 5.53 seen among others. The admissions model, designed to predict first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours, estimated a 48% probability that you would fail the bar—high enough to tip the decision toward rejecting your application, as the school prioritizes candidates likely to pass on their first try. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your LSAT score, which had the strongest negative pull compared to typical high performers. On a more positive note, your third-year decile ranking was the most influential factor in the model's favor, strongly suggesting you'd pass, followed closely by your first-year decile, both outperforming the norm and showing real academic growth. Your undergraduate GPA and family income bracket played smaller roles, leaning slightly toward the risk of failure relative to averages, while your full-time study plan offered a minor boost aligned with the majority of successful students. Overall, while your school deciles highlighted strengths that set you apart positively from many peers, the LSAT stood out as the dominant hurdle, outweighing those gains in the model's eyes. This isn't a judgment on your potential—it's just one data-driven snapshot. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score toward the typical range, strengthening your undergraduate record if possible, and reapplying to showcase your upward trajectory. You've got the grit in your deciles to build on; many students turn predictions like this around with targeted prep. Keep pushing—we're rooting for your success.
